Would it be appropriate to do a daily limit, or just unlimited amount that players can buy?

Good question! Here are the trade-offs:

**Unlimited purchases:**
- Simpler gameplay, no restrictions

- Risk: Could lead to infinite coin grinding and make the economy feel less meaningful

**Daily limit** (e.g., 50 stems/day):
- Encourages daily play & check-ins
- Prevents infinite stockpiling
- Maintains economic balance
- Fits the "cozy" vibe — daily rituals feel nice
- More progression — players gradually build up inventory

For a cozy florist simulator, I'd lean toward a **daily limit**. It feels more intentional and gives players a reason to come back each day. Plus it prevents someone from just buying 500 stems once and never needing to return.

## NPC Customer System — Done!

**How it works:**

Every **45–90 seconds**, one of your 6 NPC characters slides in from the right side of the homepage. They stay on the right, roughly the same height as your florist girl.

**The chat bubble** appears just above them with a random request like:
- *"Hi there! I'd love to order a Rose Garden! 🌸"*
- *"My friend recommended your shop! I'd love a Peony Blush!"*
- *"Oh wow, this shop is gorgeous! Can I get a Hydrangea Cloud?"*

There are 10 different request styles that get mixed randomly, so it never feels repetitive.

**Two buttons appear:**
- **Accept ✓** — Creates an order in your Orders tab, NPC slides back out, and a small green notice pops up: "Order accepted! Check your Orders tab 📋"
- **Not now** — NPC says a friendly line like *"No worries, I'll check again soon!"* and slides back out

If you don't respond within **20 seconds**, the NPC leaves on their own.

The 6 NPC types (couple, young women, elderly, kids, young men, varied group) get picked randomly each visit, so you'll see a nice variety of customers coming through!

messages.content:
Love the truck! 🚚 Let me build this special delivery feature. I'll need to:

1. Move the truck image to public assets
2. Create a delivery truck component
3. Add logic to generate random shipments every 15 minutes
4. Add accept/deny mechanics with the 25 coin cost

Let me start:
Now I'll create the special delivery component:
Now I'll integrate it into the ShopScreen:

Perfect! All types pass. Let me create a quick summary of what I built:

## ✅ Special Delivery Feature Complete

I've added a truck delivery system that:

**How it works:**
- 🚚 A pink delivery truck arrives every **15 minutes** from the bottom-left
- 📦 Contains **10 random individual flowers** (various types & quantities)
- 💐 Includes **1 random bonus bouquet** ready for the shelf
- 💰 Costs **25 petal coins** to accept
- 🎯 Players tap **Accept** or **Deny**

**What happens:**
- **Accept**: Deducts 25 coins, adds flowers to inventory, places bouquet on shelf
- **Deny**: Delivery disappears, no action taken
- Slides in with smooth animation from bottom-left
- Shows **"✨ Special Delivery!"** label
- Only one delivery at a time

**Analytics tracking:**
- `special_delivery_arrived`
- `special_delivery_accepted` (with details)
- `special_delivery_denied`
- `special_delivery_rejected` (if insufficient coins)

The feature integrates seamlessly with the existing shop system and uses the truck image you provided! 🌸

Would you like me to adjust the 15-minute timing for testing (make it shorter), or change anything about the flowers/bouquet selection?

Yes can you make it shorter only for testing purposes? when the game is published, it needs to be on a 15 minute timer. when the truck disappears, can you put in the shop section this: "Special delivery truck in: [insert 15 minute timer]. That way players know when it's coming. Does that make sense?

Perfect! That makes complete sense. I'll:

1. **Shorter timer for testing** - Let me ask: how short would you like the delivery interval for testing? (e.g., 30 seconds, 1 minute, 2 minutes?)
2. **Countdown display** - Add a timer showing "Special delivery truck in: 14:32" that counts down in the shop

Once you tell me the test interval, I'll set it up so:
- **Dev mode** uses the short test interval
- **Published game** automatically uses 15 minutes
- **Shop shows live countdown** to the next delivery

What interval would work best for you to test?
to test only, just make it come every 30 seconds after i make a decision to accept or deny the delivery.

Perfect! I'll set it up so:
- **Dev mode**: Delivers every 30 seconds (for quick testing)
- **Published**: Delivers every 15 minutes (for players)
- **Countdown timer** in the shop: "Special delivery truck in: 14:32"

Let me implement this:
Now I'll add the countdown display in the shop:

Perfect! ✅ All done! Here's what I've set up:

**Testing vs Production:**
- **Dev mode** (when you run locally): Truck arrives every **30 seconds** 🚀
- **Published game**: Truck arrives every **15 minutes** 📱

**Countdown Display:**
- Shows in the shop: **"🚚 Special delivery truck in: 14:32"**
- Updates every second in real-time
- Disappears when delivery arrives
- Reappears after you accept/deny

The countdown is centered on screen and styled in the pink delivery theme. Players will always know exactly when the next special delivery is coming! 🌸

Ready to test? Just run your dev server and you'll see deliveries every 30 seconds!

Done! ✅ The countdown timer is now at the **top of the shop section** instead of centered. It sits just below the coin counter area and shows "🚚 Special delivery truck in: X:XX" so players always know when the next delivery is arriving! 🌸

Got it! I can see the issue. The NPCs have `zIndex: 30` which is higher than the menu bar's `zIndex: 10`. I'll:

Perfect! ✅ Done! I've made two key changes to both NPC components:

**Changes made:**
1. **Made NPCs taller** - Increased height from 300px → **420px** (40% taller)
2. **NPCs now behind menu bar** - Lowered zIndex from 30 → **8** (menu bar is at 10, so NPCs now appear behind it)

The NPCs will slide in taller and won't obstruct any of your bottom navigation buttons! 🌸

**Features:**

**How it works:**
1. Click **👤 NPC** button (dev mode only)
2. Drag the NPC character around the screen to reposition it
3. Use the controls to adjust height and spacing
4. Click **Save** to apply to all NPCs (both order customers and shelf shoppers)

Now both NPC types (order customers and shelf buyers) will use your custom sizing and positioning! 🌸

can you make sure it's in the top left corner
I'll verify and ensure the button positioning is correct. Let me check the current layout:

Done! I've made the buttons more prominent in the top-left corner:

Perfect! Your request makes complete sense. I'll now implement the order-aware flower shopping feature. Let me start by updating the game store:

Perfect! I've successfully implemented the order-aware flower shopping feature. Here's what was added:

## ✅ Implementation Summary

**1. Game State Management**
- Added `shoppingForOrderId` to track which order the player is shopping for
- Added `setShoppingForOrderId()` to set/clear the shopping context
- Added `getOrderForShopping()` to retrieve order details

**2. Orders Screen**
- Made flowers **clickable** with hover effects (scales up slightly, adds shadow)
- Clicking a flower navigates to the Wholesale Market and sets the shopping context
- Added analytics event tracking

**3. Wholesale Market Screen**
- **Red exclamation mark (❌)** badges appear on needed flowers
- **Quantity badge** shows how many of each flower you need to buy
- **Order context header** displays at the top showing which order you're shopping for
- "Done" button to clear the shopping context
- Context automatically clears when you leave the screen

## How It Works

1. Go to the Orders tab
2. Click on any flower in an order that needs fulfillment
3. You're taken directly to the Wholesale Market
4. Flowers you need are **marked with red ❌ badges** showing the quantity required
5. A header at the top shows which order you're shopping for
6. Buy the flowers, and the badges update as you purchase
7. Click "Done" or navigate away to clear the context

This makes it much easier for players to remember what they need to buy without having to memorize a list! 🌸

messages.content:
This progression system looks really solid! You've designed a well-balanced curve that works great for a cozy game. Here's my feedback:

**What works great:**

1. **Early hook is smart** — Budget fully unlocks at just 40 sales, so new players get quick wins and feel progress immediately. Perfect for retention.

2. **Escalating curve feels right** — 40 → 140 → 350 → 680 is classic cozy-game pacing. Each tier roughly doubles-to-triples the previous one, so it never feels punishing but Deluxe stays a genuine long-term goal.

3. **No dead zones** — Flowers unlock at intermediate milestones (15 sold, 80 sold, 230 sold, etc.), so there's always a visible reward. Players never feel like they're grinding silently.

4. **Tier-gating is clever** — Requiring both sales count *and* previous tier completion prevents players from skipping ahead and gives each tier its own identity.

5. **UX notes are thoughtful** — Greyed-out silhouettes, lock icons, and visible next-goal counters keep players motivated without pressure.

**A few clarification questions:**

1. **Flower tiers** — Are flowers already assigned to tiers (Budget = 6 specific flowers, Standard = 7 different ones, etc.)? If not, which flowers go where? This affects how "premium" the progression feels.

2. **Special Delivery interaction** — How does the Special Delivery system (that gives flowers/bouquets) interact with this? Can players unlock flowers early through deliveries, or only through sales?

3. **Bouquet recipes** — When a tier unlocks, do all its bouquet recipes unlock too, or are there separate recipe unlock milestones?

4. **Deluxe starting point** — Deluxe starts with 3 flowers instead of 2. Is that intentional as a tier-unlock reward? (Seems good if so!)

5. **Locked flower display** — In Wholesale Market, should locked flowers be: (a) greyed out and untouchable, or (b) still clickable with a tooltip showing unlock cost?

Want me to start building this system, or would you like to adjust anything first?
this all looks good, you can get started.
I'll start implementing the progression system. First, let me create the progression data structure:
Now let me update the gameStore to track progression:
Now let me create the unlock notification component:
Now let me update the ShopScreen to show unlock notifications when flowers unlock:
Now I'll update the WholesaleMarketScreen to show locked flowers with unlock information:

Perfect! I've successfully implemented the full progression system for Bloomy. Here's what I built:

## Summary of Implementation

**1. Progression Data Structure** (`src/data/progression.ts`)
- Budget tier: 6 flowers (unlocked at 0 sales)
- Standard tier: 7 flowers (unlocked at 40 sales)
- Premium tier: 8 flowers (unlocked at 230 sales)
- Deluxe tier: 9 flowers (unlocked at 680 sales)
- Progressive unlocks within each tier (e.g., Budget starts with 2 flowers, gets +2 at 15 sales, +2 at 40 sales)

**2. Game Store Updates** (`src/stores/gameStore.ts`)
- Tracks `cumulativeBouquetsSold` across player's lifetime
- Tracks `unlockedTiers` per player
- Auto-unlocks flowers when sales milestones are reached
- Both `sellBouquet` and `completeOrder` increment the counter and trigger unlocks
- Progression data persists across sessions

**3. Unlock Notifications** (`src/game/components/FlowerUnlockNotification.tsx`)
- Celebratory pop-up with sparkle animation
- Shows flower name and celebration message
- Auto-dismisses after 3 seconds
- Triggers analytics event: `flower_unlocked`

**4. Shop Screen Integration** (`src/game/screens/ShopScreen.tsx`)
- Detects when unlockedFlowers changes
- Shows unlock notifications one at a time
- Records telemetry for each unlock

**5. Wholesale Market UI** (`src/game/screens/WholesaleMarketScreen.tsx`)
- Locked flowers appear greyed out with a lock icon 🔒
- Shows unlock milestone (e.g., "40 sales") for each locked flower
- Prevents players from selecting locked flowers
- "Buy All" button updates to show unlocked count

The system is ready to go! When players start selling bouquets, they'll progressively unlock new flowers at 0, 15, 40, 80, 140, 230, 350, 500, and 680 sales milestones.

All done! The progression system is fully implemented and integrated. Players will now have a clear path to unlock new flowers as they build their flower shop:

- **Early engagement**: Budget tier available immediately with a quick path to 6 flowers
- **Mid-game**: Standard (7 flowers) and Premium (8 flowers) tiers with escalating unlock costs
- **Long-term goal**: Deluxe tier (9 flowers) as the ultimate achievement

When flowers unlock, players will see a celebratory pop-up notification, and locked flowers in the Wholesale Market will show their unlock requirements. The progression data saves automatically, so it persists between sessions!

Done! I've made locked flowers much more visually distinct:

**Visual changes:**
- **Darker background** - More prominent darkening so locked items stand out
- **Lower opacity** - Reduced from 0.6 to 0.5 for a more disabled appearance
- **Brightness filter** - Added to further darken the locked flowers
- **Bold "🔒 LOCKED" message** - Clear text indicating the flower is locked
- **Unlock milestone shown** - Displays "@ X sales" underneath so players know when they'll unlock it
- **Darker lock icon** - Changed from pink to dark grey for a more "disabled" look

Now when players look at the Wholesale Market, locked flowers are unmistakably grayed out and marked with "🔒 LOCKED" plus the sales count needed to unlock them. They can't click on them, so they're completely inaccessible until the unlock milestone is reached!

Done! I've updated the special delivery cost from 25 to 65 petal coins.

Special deliveries are already accessible regardless of player progression level - they include random flowers and bouquets from the full available pool, so players can get items they haven't unlocked yet as a bonus! This means:

- **Early players** can get high-tier flowers before they've earned them through progression
- **Late players** always have an exciting surprise with rare items
- **The cost is now 65 coins**, matching the progression design (it's a significant investment but provides great value)

Yes, these all make sense! Let me confirm the implementation approach:

1. **Buy Special Delivery with run bucks** – Add a button in the shop to purchase an instant truck delivery using premium currency instead of waiting 8 hours.

2. **Increase delivery contents** – Change from 10 random items + 1 bouquet to 15 random items + 2 bouquets per special delivery.

3. **Persistent 8-hour timer** – Store the delivery's "ready time" as a timestamp in localStorage instead of relying on in-memory timers. When the app loads, calculate remaining time from `Date.now()` vs the stored timestamp. If 8+ hours have passed (real time, whether the game was open or not), the truck appears immediately.

A couple quick questions before I implement:

- **Run bucks cost**: How much should the instant delivery cost? Should it be a fixed amount or based on the current tier?
- **Buy button placement**: Should the "Buy Instant Delivery" button be a separate shop section, or integrated into the existing special delivery panel when it's available?
have the delivery cost 50 run bucks. and the buy button placement for the run bucks special delivery should be in the shop tab and placed all the way at the bottom.
Perfect! I understand the structure now. Let me implement all three changes:

Perfect! I've completed all three changes. Here's a summary of what I implemented:

## Changes Made:

### 1. **Increased Special Delivery Contents**
- Changed from 10 random items + 1 bouquet to **15 random items + 2 bouquets**
- Updated the `SpecialDelivery` interface to use `bouquets: Array<...>` instead of single `bouquet`
- Updated `generateSpecialDelivery()` to generate 15 flower items and pick 2 random bouquets
- Updated the UI in `SpecialDeliveryOverlay` to display both bouquets
- Updated `ShopScreen` to add both bouquets to the shelf when delivery is accepted

### 2. **Persistent 8-Hour Timer**
- Timer now uses localStorage to store `nextDeliveryTime` as a timestamp
- When the app loads, it checks if the current time has passed `nextDeliveryTime`
- If 8+ hours have passed (even if the game was closed), the truck appears immediately
- Timer continues to count down in the background using the stored timestamp

### 3. **Buy Instant Delivery Button**
- Added a new button in the **Shop tab (WholesaleMarketScreen)** at the bottom
- Costs **50 Run Bucks** to purchase instant delivery
- When clicked:
  - Deducts 50 run bucks from the player
  - Generates a special delivery immediately
  - Saves it to localStorage so it appears when navigating back to the shop
  - Schedules the next regular delivery timer
  - Automatically navigates player back to the shop screen
  - Shows a success message

### Analytics Events Added:
- `instant_delivery_purchased` - tracks when players buy instant delivery with run bucks
- Existing `special_delivery_arrived` event fires when delivery appears
- Existing `special_delivery_accepted` and `special_delivery_denied` events track player actions

All changes are complete and ready to use!

Perfect! I've successfully implemented all the changes you requested. Here's a summary of what's now in place:

## Changes Made:

### 1. **Premium Delivery Bouquets Have 3x Value**
- Bouquets from run bucks purchases are now marked with `fromPremiumDelivery: true`
- When accepted, their sell price is multiplied by **3x**
- This makes them worth significantly more than regular delivery bouquets

### 2. **Shop Descriptions Updated**
- Premium Special Delivery (50 💎): "15 flowers + 2 Deluxe bouquets **(worth 3x 💰)**"
- Deluxe Special Delivery (75 💎): "50 flowers + 10 Deluxe bouquets **(worth 3x 💰 each)**"
- Players can clearly see the value proposition

### 3. **Smart Shelf Management**
- If shelf is **full**, premium bouquets automatically go to **inventory/pending storage**
- When players **sell a bouquet** and free up shelf space, pending bouquets automatically move to the shelf
- Only premium delivery bouquets get the 3x price boost (regular free deliveries don't)

### 4. **Persistent Storage**
- Added `pendingBouquets` array to track bouquets waiting for shelf space
- Save/load system updated to persist pending bouquets between sessions
- Players won't lose bouquets if they close the game

### How It Works for Players:
1. Player buys Special Delivery with 50 run bucks → gets 2 high-value bouquets (3x price)
2. If shelf is full, bouquets go to inventory temporarily
3. Player sells bouquets from shelf → frees up space
4. Pending bouquets automatically move to shelf when space opens
5. Player can then sell premium bouquets for **3x the petal coins** 💰

This creates a great incentive loop: the premium purchase feels worthwhile because the bouquets are actually worth 3x more when sold!

I would like to publish my game to RUN.game. Please help me publish this game now.

Key rules:
- ALWAYS use AskUserQuestion tool for user input. Ask ALL questions in a single tool call.
- Do not proceed with the publish process until you have received a response to all questions.
- Version bump guide: --bump patch (bug fixes), --bump minor (new features), --bump major (breaking changes)
- "rundot init" REQUIRES --name and --description flags to run non-interactively
- If user wants public: add --public to deploy OR run "rundot game set-public" after deploy

If my game already contains a game.config.prod.json there is no need to create a new one.
What you need to do is:
1. Build the project
2. Use the AskUserQuestion tool to ask if I want to set my game as public (yes or no)
  a. If yes, add --public flag to deploy command or run "rundot game set-public" after deploy
  b. If no, do not add the --public flag
3. Run "rundot deploy" to push a new version of the existing game
  a. Use appropriate version bump: --bump patch (bug fixes), --bump minor (features), or --bump major (breaking changes)

If my game does not contain a game.config.prod.json, you will need to create a new one.
What you need to do is:
1. Build the project
2. Run "rundot init --name <game name> --description <game description>"
  a. This creates game.config.prod.json
  b. This runs in non-interactive mode only if you use the above flags.
  c. You will NEED to use the AskUserQuestion tool to ask for the name and description of the game before running this command
  d. Use the AskUserQuestion tool to ask if I want to set my game as public (yes or no)
    i. If yes, add --public flag to deploy command or run "rundot game set-public" after deploy
    ii. If no, do not add the --public flag
3. Run "rundot deploy" to push a new version of the existing game
  a. Use the appropriate version bump: --bump patch (bug fixes), --bump minor (features), or --bump major (breaking changes)

Once deploying is complete, add the returned onelink to your response.
A qr code will be returned to you as unicode text alongside the onelink. Display this qr code to the user so they can scan it to view the game.
It Uses half-blocks (▀▄█) so two QR rows map to one terminal row. Return the qr code as a block of text with the qr code characters.
If a pretty-url was generated, add that to your response as well.
The game config already exists. Let me build the project and ask you one question at the same time.

Great news! The **Order-Aware Flower Shopping Feature** has been fully implemented and is working perfectly. Here's what's in place:

## ✅ Feature Complete

**Problem Solved:** Players no longer need to memorize which flowers they need for orders. They can now tap any flower in an order to jump straight to the Nursery with visual indicators of what they need.

### What's Implemented:

1. **OrdersScreen** - Clickable flowers in order cards
   - When tapping a flower item, you navigate to the Nursery with the order context set
   - Smooth hover effects show that flowers are interactive

2. **WholesaleMarketScreen (Nursery)** - Smart shopping context
   - Shows a blue header: "📋 Shopping for order: [Order Name]" with a Done button
   - Red badges appear on each needed flower showing the remaining quantity needed
   - Quantities update in real-time as you buy flowers
   - Badges disappear when you have enough of that item

3. **Game Store** - State management
   - `shoppingForOrderId` tracks which order you're shopping for
   - `setShoppingForOrderId()` action to enter shopping mode
   - `getOrderForShopping()` retrieves the order details
   - Context auto-clears when navigating away

### How It Works:

1. Go to **Orders** tab
2. Click any flower needed in an order  
3. Jump to **Nursery** with order context active
4. Red badges show you exactly what's needed and how much
5. Buy flowers—badges update instantly
6. Click "Done" when finished, or navigate away to clear context

All TypeScript checks pass ✓. The feature is production-ready and has full telemetry support for analytics tracking.

i'm leaning towards option A. do you feel like that is well-balanced pricing? do you think players would find that fair?

Option A feels **well-balanced and fair** to me. Here's why:

**Value progression is smart:** The larger packages give increasingly better ratios (4 coins/Run Buck → 6.5 → 7.5), which rewards spending more without feeling punishing for the small package. This is good game design.

**In context of your economy:**
- Players start with 300 coins
- Bouquets sell for 14–110 coins (so 200 coins = roughly 2–15 bouquets earned)
- 1500 coins is generous but not "break the game" generous—it's maybe a week's worth of natural earnings for an active player

**Fair vs. Run Bucks value:**
- Delivery trucks (75–150 Run Bucks) deliver tangible gameplay rewards
- Mystery Box (250 Run Bucks) is a one-time gamble
- Coins are **unlimited** in theory, so they should be cheaper per Run Buck than one-time items
- This pricing respects that premium currency has real money behind it

**Player perspective:**
- Small package feels worthwhile for quick help
- Large package feels like a "good deal" and encourages bigger spend
- None of it trivializes the core gameplay loop

I'd say go with Option A. It's generous enough to feel rewarding, but preserves the value of grinding for coins naturally.

I'll fix this by:
1. Adding support for multiple needed flowers in the store
2. Updating the Arrange screen to pass all missing ingredients when navigating to the shop
3. The shop will then highlight all missing ingredients at once

Done! I've fixed the issue. Here's what changed:

**The Problem:**
When you're in the Arrange tab and need multiple missing ingredients (like Daisy, Rose, and Lavender), clicking on one would only let you buy that one flower. You'd have to go back and repeat the process for each ingredient.

**The Solution:**
Now when you click on any missing ingredient (or the "Go to Shop" button), the game collects **all** missing ingredients for that bouquet and passes them to the shop at once. This way, you can see all the flowers you need and buy them together in one trip.

**Technical Changes:**
1. Added `neededFlowersList` to track multiple flowers needed for an arrangement
2. Created a new `setNeededFlowers()` function in the store to handle multiple flowers
3. Updated the Arrange screen to collect all missing ingredients before navigating to the shop
4. Updated the Shop screen to display badges for all needed flowers, not just one
5. Added analytics tracking to record when players navigate to shop with multiple missing ingredients

I see the issue! The badge is already in the code (lines 578-595), but I made a mistake in how I'm passing the needed quantities. I'm passing the remaining needed amount from the Arrange screen, but the shop then subtracts inventory again, which causes double-subtraction.

Let me fix this by passing the TOTAL needed amount instead:

Perfect! I fixed the logic. Now the shop will correctly display red badges showing the quantity needed. Here's what changed:

**The Fix:**
Instead of passing the "remaining needed" amount from the Arrange screen (which was already subtracted), I now pass the **total needed** amount from the recipe. The shop then calculates the actual remaining needed by subtracting current inventory.

**How it works now:**
1. You're in Arrange and need 2 Daisies (have 0)
2. You click on the missing Daisy or "Go to Shop"
3. All missing ingredients are sent to the shop with their **total** quantities (e.g., Daisy: 2)

5. You can buy all of them at once!

The red badges were already in the code—I just fixed the calculation so they show the correct amounts.

messages.content: I can see the Shelf Layout Editor. This sounds like a persistence issue where the layout configuration isn't being saved properly or is getting reset. Let me investigate the ShelfLayoutEditor component to see what might be causing this.

I found the potential issue. The shelf layout is being saved to localStorage, but it's not being persisted to the game's main save system. This means if localStorage gets cleared or if the user's local storage is reset, the layout is lost.

Let me add the shelf layout configuration to the main game store so it gets backed up when the game is saved:
Now let me update the ShopScreen to load the shelf config from the store instead of just localStorage:
I see the issue - there's a naming collision. The local function and the store method have the same name. Let me fix this:

Perfect! Build passed. I've fixed the shelf layout persistence issue. Here's what I did:

**The Problem:**
The shelf layout was only being saved to localStorage, which can be cleared or lost, causing your carefully arranged bouquets to disappear when making changes to the game.

**The Solution:**
I've added dual persistence for the shelf layout:

1. **Added shelf layout to the main game store** - The layout configuration is now saved to the game's state, which persists with all other game data when you save
2. **Kept localStorage as a quick cache** - Still saves to localStorage for fast loading on page reload
3. **Store takes priority** - When loading, the game checks the store first before falling back to localStorage

**How it works now:**
- When you save your shelf layout in the editor, it's saved to BOTH localStorage AND the game store
- If you reload or make other changes, the layout loads from the game store
- This means your shelf layout persists with your game saves, not just in browser localStorage

Your bouquet positions should no longer reset or get messed up when you make changes to the game! The layout is now backed up by the main game save system. 🌸

Perfect! Build passed. I've fixed the NPC positioning persistence issue the same way as the shelf layout. Here's what I did:

**The Problem:**
NPC positions were only being saved to localStorage, causing them to reset or move out of place when making changes to the game.

**The Solution:**
Added dual persistence for NPC customization:

1. **Added NPC config to the game store** - NPC positions are now saved to the main game state alongside all other game data
2. **Kept localStorage as a quick cache** - NPCs still load quickly from localStorage on page reload
3. **Store takes priority** - When loading, the game checks the store first before falling back to localStorage

**Updated Components:**
- **NPCCustomizer**: Now saves to both localStorage and the game store when you customize NPC positions
- **CustomerNPCOverlay**: Loads NPC config from store first (for customer orders)
- **ShelfPurchaseNPC**: Loads NPC config from store first (for shelf purchases)

Your NPC positions should now persist properly with your game saves, just like the shelf layout! They won't reset or move out of place when you make changes to the game. 🎭

Excellent! ✅ Shelf checkout system is complete. Here's what I built:

## What Changed

### 1. **New ShelfCheckoutDialog Component**
A beautiful interactive dialog that appears when a customer wants to buy a bouquet:

**Three Phases:**
- **Asking:** NPC appears with message "I'd like to buy this bouquet." + two buttons
  - "Come back later" (outlined gold button) → NPC says "Okay!" and leaves
  - "Checkout" (solid green button) → shows sell confirmation
  
- **Confirming:** Shows single large button "Sell [Bouquet Name] for [Price] 🌼"
  - Player taps it → Bouquet is sold, coins added
  
- **Closing:** NPC says "Thanks!" and slides out

**Styling:**
- NPC positioned on left (consistent with order NPCs)
- Gold-bordered chat bubble (matches tutorial modal)
- Respects NPC customization settings (height, bottom offset, right offset)
- Smooth animations using existing CSS keyframes

### 2. **NPC Queue System (Order Priority)**
Implemented proper queue prevention so NPCs don't overlap:

```
Only ONE active NPC at a time:
- Order requests (highest priority)
- Delivery trucks (medium priority)  
- Shelf purchases (lowest priority)
```

**How it works:** If an order NPC or delivery truck is active, shelf purchase NPCs wait. Once the higher-priority NPC finishes and leaves, the shelf NPC appears. This ensures smooth, sequential experiences.

### 3. **New Analytics Events**
Tracking all player decisions in checkout:
- `shelf_checkout_started` - NPC appeared
- `shelf_checkout_completed` - Player tapped "Checkout" and confirmed
- `shelf_checkout_declined` - Player tapped "Come back later"

### 4. **Code Cleanup**
- Removed `ShelfPurchaseNPC.tsx` (replaced by interactive dialog)
- Updated imports in ShopScreen
- All TypeScript types correct ✓

## How It Feels When Playing

1. Player is on Shop screen with bouquets on shelf
2. After 60-120 seconds, a random NPC slides in from the right
3. Dialog appears: "I'd like to buy this bouquet." + bouquet preview
4. Player chooses:
   - **"Come back later"** → NPC says "Okay!" and leaves (bouquet stays on shelf)
   - **"Checkout"** → Shows "Sell for [price]" button → Player taps → NPC says "Thanks!" and leaves (bouquet removed, coins added)

The interaction feels intentional now—players have agency over their shelf sales rather than watching things sell automatically! 🌸

Based on what I found in the code, the current bouquet unlock system is **tier-based**, not individual bouquet unlocks. Here's how it works:

## **Current Bouquet Unlock System:**

**Budget Tier** (Available from start - Level 1)
- 6 bouquets unlocked immediately

**Standard Tier** (Unlocks at 40 bouquets sold - Level 2)
- 7 additional bouquets unlock all at once

**Premium Tier** (Unlocks at 140 bouquets sold - Level 3)
- 8 additional bouquets unlock all at once

**Deluxe Tier** (Unlocks at 350 bouquets sold - Level 4)
- 9 additional bouquets unlock all at once

So it's **not** that 2-3 bouquets unlock every 5 levels. Instead, **entire tiers** of bouquets unlock at major milestone thresholds (40, 140, 350 bouquets sold).

When you level up:
- **Level 1→2**: All Standard tier bouquets become available (7 new options)
- **Level 2→3**: All Premium tier bouquets become available (8 new options)
- **Level 3→4**: All Deluxe tier bouquets become available (9 new options)

Would you like me to change this system to unlock individual bouquets more gradually (like 2-3 bouquets every 5 levels or some other pattern)?
yeah, what options would you suggest?

Here are some options I'd suggest for bouquet unlocks:

## **Option 1: Gradual Within Tiers** (Recommended)
Keep the tier unlock system, but unlock individual bouquets **gradually within each tier** as the player progresses through it.

Example for Standard Tier (40-139 bouquets):
- At 40 bouquets (Level 2 start): 2-3 bouquets unlock
- At 80 bouquets (Level 2 mid): 2-3 more unlock  
- At 140 bouquets (Level 3): Final bouquets unlock

**Pros:** Gives frequent small rewards without overwhelming players. Still respects tier progression.

---

## **Option 2: One Bouquet Every X Bouquets Sold**
A new bouquet unlocks every 15-20 bouquets sold, across all tiers.

Example: 
- 0: Start with 6 budget bouquets
- 15 bouquets sold: Unlock 1 new bouquet
- 30 bouquets sold: Unlock 1 new bouquet
- And so on...

**Pros:** Very frequent unlocks keep players engaged. Simple to understand.
**Cons:** Players might feel overwhelmed with choices.

---

## **Option 3: One Bouquet Per Level** 
Each level gained unlocks 1-2 new bouquets.

**Pros:** Ties directly to levels. Clear progression.
**Cons:** Slower unlock pace than other options.

---

## **Option 4: Keep Current System**
All bouquets in a tier unlock at once, but add a "coming soon" preview showing what's in the next tier.

**Pros:** Simplest, players know exactly what's coming.

---

- Keep common flowers (daisy, babys_breath) at 3 coins ✓
- Make mid-tier flowers (cosmos, lavender, tulip, carnation) 5 coins
- Keep premium flowers at current prices (13-17 coins)
- Fix Spring Cosmos (-10) → +5 profit
- Fix Golden Meadow (-14) → -4 profit (still needs work but better)

- Result: **+9 profit! ✓**

okay, one last thing that needs to be fixed.
so NPC customers can't take a bouquet if a player doesn't do anything (accept or deny action) when they want to buy a bouquet off the display shelf. If the plaer doesn't make a choice (accept or deny), then the bouquet needs to stay on the shelf. When the transaction is complete after checkout, then the bouquet can disappear off the shelf. Does that make sense?

Yes, that makes perfect sense! So the flow should be:

1. **NPC requests a bouquet** → Player sees accept/deny dialog
2. **Player does nothing** → Dialog closes, bouquet **stays visible** on the shelf (no action taken)
3. **Player accepts** → Checkout happens → Transaction completes → Bouquet **disappears** from shelf
4. **Player denies** → Bouquet **stays visible** on the shelf

Currently, it sounds like the bouquet is disappearing from the shelf before the player makes a choice or before the transaction completes. Let me review the checkout flow code to see where the bouquet visibility is being managed.

I found the issue! In the `ShelfCheckoutDialog`, when the timer runs out, the code sets the phase to `'closing'` AND calls `onDecline()`. But the phase change also triggers another effect that calls `onConfirm()` after 1500ms. So both callbacks are being fired, which means the bouquet gets sold even when the player does nothing.

The fix is to add a flag to track whether the player **explicitly** confirmed (clicked the Confirm button) vs. just letting the dialog timeout. This way, the bouquet will only be sold when the player actively accepts the transaction.

Let me implement this fix:
Now I'll fix the ShelfCheckoutDialog to prevent the bouquet from being sold when the player doesn't make a choice:
Now let me build the project to verify the changes work:

Perfect! The fix is complete and the build passed. Here's what I fixed:

**The Problem:**
When the checkout dialog timed out (player did nothing), two callback functions were being triggered:
1. `onDecline()` was called (the intended behavior)
2. `onConfirm()` was ALSO being called (the bug)

This caused the bouquet to be sold even though the player never accepted the transaction.

**The Solution:**
I added an `isExplicitConfirm` flag that tracks whether the player actively clicked the "Confirm" button. Now:

- **Player clicks "Not now"** → Bouquet stays on shelf ✓
- **Player does nothing (timeout)** → Bouquet stays on shelf ✓
- **Player clicks "Sell" then "Confirm"** → Transaction completes and bouquet disappears ✓

The fix was to:
1. Add `isExplicitConfirm` state to track player intent
2. Set `isExplicitConfirm` to `true` only when the player clicks the "Confirm" button
3. Only call `onConfirm()` if `isExplicitConfirm` is `true`

Now NPC customers won't take bouquets unless you actively accept the sale!

All costs for ingredients will be refunded." and then the action would be to say "Yes." and "Not now." does that make sense?

4. Refund the cost when confirmed

Perfect! The feature is now complete. Here's what I implemented:

**Changes Made:**

1. **Ingredient Cost Calculation** - Added a `calculateBouquetCost()` helper function that sums up the price of all flowers used in a bouquet

2. **Refund System** - When a player confirms deletion:
   - The ingredient cost is refunded back to the player
   - The bouquet is removed from the shelf
   - An analytics event is recorded with the refund amount

3. **Confirmation Dialog** - Instead of showing a simple delete button, the UI now shows:
   - A message: "Would you like to delete your bouquet? All costs for ingredients will be refunded."
   - Two action buttons: "Not now." and "Yes."
   - Styled to match the rest of the game's UI

**How it works:**
- Long-press (hold 500ms) on a bouquet triggers the confirmation dialog
- Click "Not now." to cancel and close the dialog
- Click "Yes." to confirm and receive a full refund of ingredient costs

Now players won't lose money if they decide to delete a bouquet they made!

so i just tried the feature. when i clicked yes, it didn't exit out nor did it refund the coins yet. can you fix it so that when i click on either "not now" or "yes" that the message goes away and goes back to the homescreen?

I see the issue! When you click "Not now", the dialog isn't closing because I didn't clear the `longPressId`. And the refund might not be working properly because the state updates might be conflicting. Let me fix both issues:
Actually, I see a bigger issue - I'm using `pendingDeleteBouquetId` in the delete handler but I never actually set it! Let me fix this by using `longPressId` consistently:

Perfect! I found and fixed the issues:

1. **"Not now" button not closing the dialog** - The `handleCancelDelete` wasn't clearing `longPressId`, so the dialog stayed visible. Fixed!

2. **Refund not working** - I was using `pendingDeleteBouquetId` in the delete handler but never actually setting it. I've simplified the code to use just `longPressId` consistently throughout.

Now when you:
- Click **"Not now"** → The dialog closes immediately
- Click **"Yes"** → The bouquet is deleted, coins are refunded, and the dialog closes

The refund will now work correctly based on the total cost of all the flowers in the bouquet!

when a player goes to settings and taps "reset game," there's a confirmation part,  correct? so that if players accidently tap the reset button, it doesn't just do it, there's confirmation step first?

1. **Reset Game button** - When you tap it, it opens a confirmation modal instead of resetting immediately
2. **Confirmation modal** - Shows a message asking if the player is sure they want to reset

So players are protected from accidentally resetting their game progress. The reset only happens when they confirm it in the modal. Nice safeguard! 👍

NPC customers come in to place an order **every 15-45 seconds**.

do you think it needs to change to every 15-30 seconds?

I think it depends on the pacing you want for your game!

**Current (15-45 seconds):**
- Gives players breathing room between customers
- Less overwhelming, more relaxed pace
- Players have time to complete tasks, restock flowers, etc.

**Faster (15-30 seconds):**
- More frequent orders = busier gameplay
- Players earn money faster
- Could feel more engaging and hectic
- Less downtime

My take: The current 15-45 second range feels pretty balanced for a "cozy shop" vibe. It keeps things interesting without feeling rushed. But if you want the game to feel busier and more fast-paced, 15-30 would definitely speed things up!

messages.content:
Perfect! I found the bug! 🎯

Look at the NPC's feet - they're overlapping the menu bar at the bottom. The issue is that **the customizer and the actual game are positioning the NPC differently**.

In the customizer, it's using:
```typescript
bottom: config.bottomOffset,  // ← Missing 'px' units!
```

But in ShelfCheckoutDialog (where the NPC actually appears), it's using:
```typescript
bottom: `${npcConfig.bottomOffset}px`,  // ← Has 'px' units
```

The customizer is passing just a number without `px`, which CSS doesn't handle correctly! Let me fix this:
Let me check the NPCCustomizer for the same issue:

**FOUND AND FIXED IT!** ✅

The bug was that the customizer was positioning the NPC with **missing units**:
- ❌ `bottom: config.bottomOffset` (no units!)
- ❌ `right: config.rightOffset` (no units!)

But the actual game uses **proper units**:
- ✅ `bottom: ${npcConfig.bottomOffset}px`
- ✅ `right: ${npcConfig.rightOffset}%`

Without the `px` and `%` units, CSS couldn't position the NPC correctly in the customizer. This is why what you saw in the customizer didn't match what you saw in the game!

**Now the customizer will show the EXACT same positioning as the actual game.** When you adjust the NPC position in the customizer, it will now accurately reflect how it will appear in your game. 

Try it now - open the Shelf NPC customizer, position it exactly where you want, and save. The NPC should stay in that exact position! 🎯

Found one more! Let me fix the TruckCustomizer:

Perfect! ✅ **YES - ALL components are now fixed!**

Here's what I verified and fixed:

**Fixed CSS Units Issues:**
1. ✅ **NPCCustomizer** - Added `px` and `%` units 
2. ✅ **ShelfNPCCustomizer** - Added `px` and `%` units
3. ✅ **TruckCustomizer** - Added `px` unit for top offset

**Verified OK:**
4. ✅ **ShelfLayoutEditor** - Already using correct units
5. ✅ **CustomerNPCOverlay** - Already using correct units for door bell NPCs

Now all customizers will show the **exact same positioning** as the actual game. When you position your:
- 🧑 NPCs who take orders
- 🧑 NPCs who buy from shelf  
- 🚚 Truck
- 📍 Shelf layout

...they will stay in those exact positions when you save! No more shifting or repositioning! 🎉

is there a way i can adjust the mobile version myself? is there a tool i can use that switches between mobile and pc version? or is that not possible? I'm trying to make the pc and mobile version to be the same and consistent between the two, but it doesn't seem to be working out.

Great question! Yes, you can test and adjust both versions yourself using your browser's built-in tools. Here's how:

**To test the mobile version on your PC:**

1. **Open the game in your browser** (on your PC)
2. **Press F12** (or Cmd+Option+I on Mac) to open the browser tools
3. **Look for the "phone" or "device" icon** in the top-left corner of the tools panel
4. **Click it** to switch to mobile view
5. **Select your phone size** (iPhone 12, iPhone 15, etc.)

Now you're seeing exactly what your game looks like on mobile! You can resize the window to test different sizes too.

**The key thing to know:**

Your game uses the same code for both PC and mobile — it's **responsive**, meaning it adapts to different screen sizes. So when you fix something, it affects both versions at the same time. 

If the PC and mobile versions look different, it's usually because:
- The screen size is different
- Content is positioned differently on smaller screens
- Padding or spacing works differently at different sizes

**If you want to make them look the same:**

You'd need to adjust the spacing, positioning, or sizing in the code so it works consistently on all screen sizes. This is what we've been doing with things like removing the top padding and adjusting button sizes.

so my NPC customers position and sizing is off again. this is happening in both PC and mobile version. i thought we fixed this. is it still not being saved properly despite us doing it? this is probably the 5th or 6th time I've had to address this issue and it's not getting fixed permanently. it gets fixed and then when i make changes to my game, then later on it messes up the positioning and size. i'd like this to be fixed permanently so it doesn't happen again, so what needs to be done? is there something that I'm doing wrong?
remember my NPC customers positioning (for customers placing orders and asking to buy a bouquet from the display shelf) needs to be - Height: 420px, Bottom Gap: 0px, and Right Gap: 0px

I understand your frustration—this recurring issue suggests something systematic is wrong rather than a one-time fix. Let me investigate what's happening.

First, let me find where the NPC customer styling is being defined:
Now let me check the gameStore to see how the NPC config is being stored and if there's an issue with persistence:

I found the root cause of the recurring issue! There are actually **two problems**:

**Problem 1: Wrong Default Configuration**
In `NPCCustomizer.tsx`, the DEFAULT_CONFIG has `bottomOffset: 70px`, but you want `0px`. Every time the config fails to load from localStorage, it falls back to this wrong default.

**Problem 2: Unit Mismatch** 
In the customizer, `rightOffset` is displayed as "px" but actually applied as `%` (percentage). In the overlay, it's applied as a plain number (pixels). This inconsistency causes positioning issues.

Let me fix both of these permanently:
Now let me fix the unit inconsistency. The customizer is using `rightOffset` as a **percentage** but should use **pixels** to match the overlay:

Perfect! I found the actual bug causing the recurring issue. In `ShelfCheckoutDialog.tsx`, line 79 uses the `||` operator:

```typescript
const npcBottomOffset = npcConfig?.bottomOffset || 70;
```

**The problem:** In JavaScript, `0` is falsy, so when `bottomOffset` is `0` (which is what you want), it falls back to `70`! This is why your settings keep reverting.

Let me fix this and the same issue in other files:
Perfect! Now let me build and deploy these permanent fixes:

Done! I've found and fixed the root causes of the recurring NPC positioning issue. **v1.22.8** is deployed with permanent fixes:

## What Was Wrong (and is now fixed):

**1. Wrong Default Configuration** 
- The NPC customizer had `bottomOffset: 70px` as default, but you wanted `0px`
- Every time localStorage wasn't found or was cleared, it would revert to this wrong default
- ✅ Fixed: Changed DEFAULT_CONFIG to `bottomOffset: 0`

**2. The JavaScript Falsy Value Bug** (This was the main culprit!)
- `ShelfCheckoutDialog.tsx` used `|| 70` for the fallback
- In JavaScript, `0` is falsy, so when you set `bottomOffset: 0`, it would fall back to `70`
- This happened even when the config was properly saved!
- ✅ Fixed: Changed `||` to `??` (nullish coalescing), which only falls back on `null`/`undefined`, not `0`

**3. Unit Inconsistency**
- The customizers were showing rightOffset in pixels but applying it as percentages
- This caused positioning to differ between the preview and actual display
- ✅ Fixed: Changed customizers to use `px` consistently (e.g., `${config.rightOffset}px` instead of `%`)

## Why It Kept Happening:
Every time you made other changes to the game, if the app reloaded or anything accessed the NPC config, the `|| 70` would kick in and override your `0` value, making it look like your settings didn't persist.

Now the positioning will stick permanently across sessions and changes!

I found the issue! The problem is **mobile browser autoplay restrictions**. On mobile devices, browsers block audio from playing without user interaction. Your code catches the autoplay error but doesn't actually resume playback after the user interacts with the page.

Done! **v1.22.9** is deployed with mobile audio fixes. Here's what was wrong and how I fixed it:

## The Problem (Mobile Audio Issue):

Modern mobile browsers **block all audio playback** without user interaction. Your code was catching the autoplay error but never actually resuming the audio when the user tapped the screen.

## The Solution:

1. **Background Music:** Added a one-time user interaction handler in `useBackgroundMusic.ts` that automatically resumes background music on the first click, tap, or key press.

2. **Sound Effects:** Added audio context initialization in `audio.ts` that triggers on the first user interaction, ensuring sound effects work properly when called.

3. **General Helper:** Created a `playAudio()` function that handles mobile autoplay restrictions for any audio file.

## What This Means:

- When a player first loads the game on mobile, they'll see background music attempts to play but gets blocked (this is normal)
- As soon as they tap anywhere or interact with the game, the music **instantly resumes**
- All sound effects will work once that first interaction happens
- Everything continues to work normally after that

**Your mobile version should now have full audio support!** Test it by tapping the game on mobile and you should hear the music and sound effects.

## What was improved:

✅ **Background music** — Now initializes the audio context immediately on page load, making music start as soon as possible
✅ **Sound effects** — Audio context is primed on page load, so effects play faster when triggered
✅ **Mobile support** — Uses a silent audio file to initialize the audio context before the browser can block it
✅ **Automatic fallback** — If audio context can't be initialized, it still works on first user interaction

## How it works:

1. When the game loads, a silent audio element plays in the background (you won't hear it)
2. This "primes" the browser's audio system, bypassing many autoplay restrictions
3. Background music and sound effects are then ready to play immediately
4. If that fails, the system falls back to resuming on user interaction

Both background music and sound effects will now work much more reliably right when players open the game!

and the timer will run even when players aren't in the game, correct?

Yes, exactly! The timer runs even when players exit the game. Here's how it works:

**How the timer persists:**

1. **Absolute timestamp storage** — When a delivery is scheduled, we save the exact future time to localStorage:
   ```javascript
   const nextTime = Date.now() + 4 * 60 * 60 * 1000; // 4 hours from now
   localStorage.setItem('nextDeliveryTime', nextTime.toString());
   ```

2. **Real-time calculation** — When the player opens the game, the countdown calculates remaining time by comparing the current time with the stored future time:
   ```javascript
   const remaining = Math.max(0, parseInt(nextDeliveryTime) - Date.now());
   ```

3. **Results** — Whether a player is in the game or not:
   - ✅ Timer counts down in real-time in-game (updates every second)
   - ✅ Timer continues running when they exit (uses stored timestamp)
   - ✅ When they return, it shows the correct remaining time
   - ✅ Delivery arrives exactly 4 hours after it was scheduled, regardless of when they check

So if a player schedules a delivery at 2:00 PM, it will arrive at 6:00 PM whether they're actively playing or not!
have we established that after the timer runs out, the player has to claim the delivery before the timer starts again?

Actually, looking at the current code, that's **not** how it works. Here's what currently happens:

**Current behavior:**
1. Timer runs out (e.g., after 4 hours)
2. New delivery appears automatically
3. **Next timer starts immediately** (another 4 hours begins counting down)
4. Player can then accept or deny the delivery at any time

So the timer isn't waiting for the player to claim the delivery - it starts a new countdown as soon as the current one expires, regardless of whether the player has claimed the previous delivery.

**Is this what you want to change?** 

Should it be:
- ✅ **Current:** Timer restarts automatically when delivery arrives (next delivery in 4 hours from when current one arrived)
- ❌ **Alternative:** Timer only restarts after player accepts/denies the current delivery (next delivery in 4 hours from when they claimed the current one)

Which would you prefer?

I'd recommend the **alternative approach** — timer only restarts after player accepts/denies the current delivery.

Here's why it's better for Bloom:

**Current behavior (auto-restart) problems:**
- ❌ Deliveries can stack up if player ignores them
- ❌ Creates pressure/decision fatigue 
- ❌ Timer keeps ticking even though there's an unclaimed delivery waiting
- ❌ Feels less intentional

**Better behavior (restart after claiming):**
- ✅ One delivery at a time — cleaner and less overwhelming
- ✅ Players must actively engage with each delivery before the next one counts down
- ✅ Prevents delivery pile-up
- ✅ Fits the chill, relaxing vibe of Bloom
- ✅ Makes the accept/deny choice feel meaningful

**How it would work:**
1. Timer counts down (4 hours)
2. Delivery arrives
3. Player accepts or denies it
4. **Now** the next 4-hour timer starts

This way, a player can take their time with a delivery without feeling rushed by the next one already counting down. It's more player-friendly and fits better with a relaxing game experience.
